humanities_role_explanation: Uses humanities disciplines (such as history, world religions, philosophy, music, and movies) as factual content domains within a multi-topic prompt set to test the general long-form factuality of language models.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only prompts and responses are used to evaluate long-form open-ended factual text generation.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates generative LLMs on long-form, open-domain generation by breaking down responses into atomic facts and verifying their accuracy (factual precision) and coverage (factual recall up to a target number of facts K).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates model factuality across 38 broad topics spanning STEM, Social Sciences, Humanities, and other areas.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Benchmarks 13 prominent LLMs across the Gemini, GPT, Claude, and PaLM-2 model families.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being evaluated are generative LLMs (though an LLM is also used as an agent in the evaluation pipeline).

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes humanities topics corresponding to ASJC areas such as History, Philosophy, Music, Religious Studies, and Visual Arts and Performing Arts (movies, architecture, celebrities).

quantitative_metrics.note: Measures performance using precision, recall, and a proposed F1@K metric, annotated automatically by the search-augmented SAFE evaluator.

english_language.note: The paper and the LongFact prompts are written in English.

summary: The paper introduces LongFact, a multi-topic dataset containing 2,280 prompts across 38 categories designed to evaluate the factuality of long-form LLM responses. It also presents SAFE, an agentic evaluation pipeline that extracts and fact-checks individual claims via Google Search, alongside F1@K, a novel metric combining factual precision and length-bounded recall.
key_findings: Larger language models generally exhibit superior long-form factuality. The automated SAFE rater agrees with crowdsourced human annotators 72% of the time, outperforms them on disagreement cases (76% to 19%), and is over 20 times cheaper.
